original_abstract: EU-US data transfers for health research remain a particularly thorny issue in view of the stringent rules of the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and the challenges related to US mass surveillance programs, particularly the manner in which US law enforcement and national security agencies can access personal data originating from the EU. Since the entry into force of the GDPR, evidence of impeded collaborations is increasing, particularly in the case of sharing data with US public institutions. The adoption of a new EU-US adequacy decision in July 2023 does not hold the promise for a long-lasting solution due to the risks of being challenged and invalidated - yet again - at the Court of Justice of the EU. As the research community is calling for answers, the new proposal for a European Health Data Space regulation may hold a key to solving some of the existing issues. In this paper, we critically discuss the current rules and outline a possible way forward for transfers between public bodies.
humanized_abstract: EU-US health research data transfer is a sensitive topic within the current context of the GDPR, and the ongoing US mass surveillance affairs. Since the entry into force of the GDPR, several examples have been reported where data-sharing activities for health research purposes have been slowed down, or even stopped, especially when the data has to be sent to the US public sector due to increased concerns on how law enforcement and national security agencies use the data sent from the EU. However, the EU-US adequacy decision approved in July 2023 is only a temporary measure, because it may be challenged before the Court of Justice of the EU and annulled, once again. Data-sharing between research activities has been hampered in the recent months, and some answers are required by the research community. An opportunity for solving some of the data-transfer issues in health research between public sector agencies is introduced by the European Health Data Space (EDHS) regulation, proposed by the European Commission (EC). In this paper, we analyse the current EU Data Protection Regulation rules and assess possible alternatives or improvements, in order to ensure high-quality data sharing for essential health research among public sector agencies.

Data-sharing between research activities has been hampered in the recent months, and some answers are required by the research community. An opportunity for solving some of the data-transfer issues in health research between public sector agencies is introduced by the European Health Data Space (EDHS) regulation, proposed by the European Commission (EC). In this paper, we analyse the current EU Data Protection Regulation rules and assess possible alternatives or improvements, in order to ensure high-quality data sharing for essential health research among public sector agencies.
